speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Pauline Wrobel. I am conducting an interview with Lola Putt, today, the 27th of November, 1996, in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia. Thank-- Lola, would you like to introduce the members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure. All right, first of all, I want to introduce my son, Harry, and his children-- and his wife and children, Diane, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jarrod, Kiarra, and Tynan. They are wonderful kids. I'm very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you like to introduce--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Garry. I met Garry six years ago. And we'll be together. And we have a wonderful time together. And he's my partner and a very, very nice man. I couldn't wish for a better one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harry, would you like to say something to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I have spoken to my mum, on a number of occasions, over the years. And she has-- has said to me what she has-- what she has been through. And it's very, very-- really very hard to-- to believe that someone could, you know, survive and come out the way she has. I know I just-- I-- I love her deeply. And I'm glad she's my mum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else would like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am very glad to learn something about the Holocaust. But it was very upsetting to hear that she was in it, that she went through it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a Holocaust center a few weeks ago. And I just realized how hard it was for everyone that was at war. It made me appreciate, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that I'm a bit older, I'm just sort of been able to understand what my grandmother went through. And I'm just proud to still have her here with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOB]

speaker: interviewer
text: Garry, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say that, as Lola said, we've been here together for a number of years, for six years. I hope that there will be 30 to go. I'm very happy with her. She's a easy person to be with. And her family-- family is very nice to me, too. Sometimes, perhaps not, but, otherwise, it doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. [? LP: ?] Oh, sorry-- Tape one with Lola Putt. Lola, where did-- where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What was the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Salonika.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-O-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband name was Putt, P-U-T-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We can move along.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born, Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 19-- 1926, in the 5th of May-- the 16th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Salonika. That was the town. That was a town, a big town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a house? Or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a house. In a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived with you in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family, my father, my mother, we were five children, four girls and a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a nice home. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had two bedrooms and a kitchen. It was not very big. But it was comfortable for us. We were a very happy family. My father had his own business. He was doing things to put halvah in. And he was doing quite very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the suburb where your house was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The suburb? What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a lot of Jewish people that lived in that suburb?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were. Yes, yes. There were a few Jewish people. But after where the Germans came in-- 1943, they came in. And they told all the Jewish people that they send us to a-- to a-- in Germany. And that they will give us house to live. They will give us jobs. They was give us money. And not to worry about it. But why they did that is because they want to trick us, so we can go peacefully.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember my grandmother for my mother. I remember my grandmother for my father. And I didn't have any grandfathers. I didn't know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What role did they play in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were good-- good-- good people, good-- good family, good-- good lovable people. We had a wonderful life. We had love. We had everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they live close by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. My grandmother was living by herself, because her husband died when she-- when my mother was born, the father died. She was-- my grandmother was a widow for a long-- for a very long time. But she didn't remarry. Those days, they never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Was there a very strong Jewish community in Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very strong. They-- they were together and were going to synagogues, you know, and-- and have Jewish functions and things like that. I remember my father used to go to, every Saturday, to the synagogue. We were not a very religious people. But every time it used to come Pesach or Yom Tishrei, we always were celebrating and enjoy ourselves. And-- and every Friday, my-- my father used to do kiddish. It was a wonderful, warm atmosphere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to my father and close to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things did you do with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my father? Well, he was always busy, my father. But when it come the weekend, we used to be together. You know, sit and-- sit and talk, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] talk about many things. Well, sometimes he used to ask me, why-- how was your schooling today? What you did? Or what did you go? It was always a understanding man. But he was busy working very hard to support the family. Can you tell me about your mother, Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a lady, a real lady. You wouldn't believe it, she was blonde. And she was a real lady. And, like I said before, she had five children. She was very busy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they brought us in a good manner. And she was always hard-working woman. Those days, that is not like today. They used to work, women, harder, you know? And to bring the children-- they-- they used to have more children then than today. You know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was a housewife. She-- she never worked. I mean, she was working at the home, with the children, to bring up the children, take them to school, bring them from school. But my father was working very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a particular day with your mother you would like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always with my mother. I wish that I would have her today. I would give anything. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I would give anything to have my mother back today and my father and my brothers, my sisters. I have one sister that she was-- we were in the-- in the camp. Not in the camp, in the-- what you call-- a ghetto in Greece. When the Germans came, 1943, they put us in a ghetto, [BACKGROUND NOISE] and the whole family together, other people together. And my sister escaped from the ghetto, because she had friends that took her away. And she was hiding for two years, in one room for two years. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, what languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- well, home, we spoke Spanish, because we belong to the Spanish Jews, you know? Sephardic, what you call it, you know? And my mother-language was Spanish. And we spoke at home Spanish. And-- and Greece was the country that we spoke Greek as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of school did you go to, Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a-- not a private school, but the school-- what you call it? Like state school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever encounter antisemitism at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes. It was in Greece. It was. It was a bit antisemitism, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you share any incidents with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] I-- I could see they didn't like us very much, because we were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the teachers treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the teachers was all right, because we had-- a few hours, we had Greek lessons. And then one hour, we had a Jewish lesson. But they were nice to us, I can say. It was just the country. I mean, the people sometimes, you know, they felt that we are Jewish, then they didn't feel that-- so happy about it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel any antisemitism-- any antisemitism from the other children in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like they used to call us evraioi. Evraioi means Jews. When I was in school, they used to call us evraioi. That's the word in Greek, Jews. But we live there for so many years, and we got used to-- to this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things did you do with your friends as a young girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a young girl, I was full of life-- full of life. I used to go out and play the skipping-- the skipping rope with the girls. We used to play on the floor with those marbles, you know? And it was a wonderful life. I mean, never-- never, never had any upsettings or worries in the world. And it was a-- a good-- good years when we were together with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other interests did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to be a dancer and a singer, because I remember, when I was a very young girl, I used to go put my mother's high-heeled shoes, with long dresses, and put lipstick on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to put-- we used to have microphone. You know those older ones, the-- the Phillips? We used to-- I used to put the-- a record. And we had a long mirror in my house. And I used to look myself in the mirror, and I used to dance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dance, I was crazy about dancing. And I wanted to sing. And I want to dance. I was very young then. And my mother my-- always said to my father, oh, I'm sure she'll become a film star one day. [LAUGHS] But my dreams didn't come true. I was very young, Pauline. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took my-- my-- my youth away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you refer to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Germans. They took my youth away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took my parents away. My best years of my life, they took away from me. And I was so young. And I need my parents. And I need my freedom. And the Germans took all away from me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The best years of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you're young, you need your parents. You need your freedom. And that, I didn't have. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You can understand that, don't you? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm sorry if I sound a bit nervous. It's not easy. How would you describe the Jewish community in Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, close. Close, happy. Very happy. I was very young, Pauline, and I didn't think about anything but-- but living, you know? Life was so youthful, you know? Since they finished me off, that-- in that way that I have to go to concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was happening in Europe in the late '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I-- I-- I was too young to-- to understand it. No. After, when I came here to Australia, I heard all about it. Because I was too young. I didn't took much notice of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first memory of danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean when? Before-- before the war? Or-- or when the war started?

speaker: interviewer
text: At any point, did you feel-- did you begin to feel that your life was in danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life was in danger only when the Germans came in, in Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1945-- 1943, I'm sorry. 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at the time, Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in school. And they told us to go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you in 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 16. And then, like I mentioned before, they told us they're going to send us away and give us everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: From school?

speaker: interviewee
text: From school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Home? They-- they told us to prepare what we have to take with us, so we can-- we can-- they sent us away, with my parents, with-- with the family, my grandmothers, both grandmothers. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the first day? When they arrived, was this-- when did this happen? When were you asked to--

speaker: interviewee
text: That when-- soon-- soon the Germans came in, they started to tell us what we have to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what date it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, Pauline.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how much after their arrival were you told to pack up?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Germans came in? Maybe a few days. Soon, they came into Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember conversations that took place between your parents and the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They explained to us that the-- this what we have to do, and that was an order. You could do nothing about it. If they tell you, you have to go, you have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about the order?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very depressing. We didn't want to leave our homes. We didn't want to leave our friends. We didn't want to leave the-- the school. We didn't want to do all of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And break the house? Everything has been taken away. How do you feel? How would you feel? Did you have Greek girlfriends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. My sister had friends that I-- she-- when she escaped from-- from the ghetto, there were friends that were not Jewish, they look after her. I can't say everybody was the same. There were some good people and bad people. Is today, too, the same thing in the whole world, [LAUGHS] whole wide world. You've got good people and bad people.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Were there-- was there a Jewish organization that started to look after the Jews in Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there-- there-- it were-- When, you mean before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. When the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the German came, no, there-- they have the same-- the same deal as we had. You were a Jew, and you have to leave. That was not a question about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you share with us what happened when you packed up your things to leave your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We packed what we could take with us, because it was not so easy. I mean, we have to carry in the hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things did you take, Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took some clothing. And we took a little bit of food, not much, because we didn't know where we're going. If they promise us such a good deal, so we didn't thought about anything. And then, we-- they put us in a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it had-- it didn't have any name. They put us there just close to the station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put-- they used to put people there every day. Every day, every day bring people, more and more and more people there. And every day, they-- yeah, they sent them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took us from home. They throw us from home. The Germans came. And they said, out. And we have to leave. And they put us to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go on-- did you go by foot or by--

speaker: interviewee
text: Foot. By foot. We went to the ghetto, all the family, together, and other Jewish people, as well. We went to this ghetto, that I said is-- was near to the station. And every day, transport people to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they put us in a-- in a-- what you call this-- in trains. When we in the ghetto, they put us in a train to go to-- to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that train is like those trains that they put horses in with a small window in the side. You know those trains that they have horses in and a small window in the side? They put us there, like herrings, too many people, much too many. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was there with my parents, with my sister, with my other two little sisters, my brother, my father. And they send us to a-- to Germany, I don't know, to Auschwitz, what you call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, when you were in the ghetto, where did you actually live in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- we were not very long there. We had a house, doesn't matter where. You-- you could just sleep in the streets. You could sleep anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sleep on the floor. We didn't have where to go. I mean, there was a place. There was no many houses around there. It was not a real ghetto like-- like Poland had. It's different. I don't know. It was different in Greece. That's what I remember. And that's what I can tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you organized food to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I-- yes, I'll tell you. We took a little bit food with us. And when they put us in this train, that they have those-- so many people in and-- and such a small window and so many people, it was shocking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, when we were traveling, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] people-- many people died, because we didn't have no food, and we didn't have no water. Many people die.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can tell you one thing. I saw this with my own eyes. One person urinated himself, and he drank it. So bad he was. They did not give us no food and no water. I remember, very well, my father took with him a bottle, an old bottle that he had a handle and-- and straw round, round the old bottles. And he took a bit of water with him when we went. But when we were traveling for a few days, we didn't have any more.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, the train stop. And my father went to the window. He took the bottle. And he put-- he saw a soldier, a German soldier standing there. And he took the bottle to give it to the-- to the German soldier to give us a bit of water. So he took his rifle, and he broke the bottle. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Smashed the bottle. He didn't want to give us any water. And that was traveling this for a few days. How did your father react to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you imagine? He want to give us water, to his children, that we were dying. How-- what can you say? Isn't it painful? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, then the train start again to go. And one day, we arrive in Auschwitz. I don't know where it was. That's what they said. And they told us to get out from the train. And there were many people lying there dead, already. And we went-- At the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the station, in the-- in-- already, in the trains, inside the trains. Because they-- they die for not having food and no water. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the bodies? Then they took it away to the crematoriums. What will they do with it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took it away?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans did, of course. Then, they told us to go down. They took women separate, children separate. No. The mothers with the children, they went together. The young people separate, because, if they have young people, they want them to-- to work for them. That's why they didn't kill us first, because they need our help. There was children and-- and-- and-- and mothers together, and old people separate, and young people, men, separate. They never put the men with women together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then we went to-- they took us to the-- the camp. And they told us to undress and shave us the heads, shave us the hair. And then they gave us uniform with the stripes, like a prisoner's. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they went and marked us those numbers, in the hand, with a needle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you repeat the numbers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's 39403. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when they did that, I saw people walking like dogs, swollen feet, swollen hands, full of lices. They look like animals. And I ask a lady, I say, what's wrong with them? What's happening to them? She said, from hunger, this is how they look. From thirst, from hunger, and this is how they become. And I got so scare. I was terrify. They were really like animals, wild people. Walk? They couldn't even stand, because they were so swollen. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, when they shave us heads and-- and the uniforms and the numbers was done, they put us in a-- a blocks, what you call barracks. They called barracks. There were many barracks there, one next to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Auschwitz. And they put us-- there were beds there, what you call beds, wooden beds, wood, just wood, no mattress and no blankets. Nothing, just wood. We were five women in one bed, sleep in one bed, with nothing on. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And once a week, they used to call us on l'appel. That means 'in attention.' It was winter. It was cold. And we were there for hours, standing on attention, to count us, to see if we-- everybody is there, nobody escape. That was every morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Lola. We'll end tape one now. Thank you.
